content:
# 1 Corinthians Chapter 13 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1CO_013_page_001.jpg

1. If I speak in the tongues of men and of angels, but have not love, I am a noisy gong or a clanging cymbal.

2. And if I have prophetic powers, and understand all mysteries and all knowledge, and if I have all faith, so as to remove mountains, but have not love, I am nothing.

3. If I give away all I have, and if I deliver up my body to boast, but have not love, I gain nothing.

4. Love is patient and kind; love does not envy or boast; it is not arrogant

5. or rude. It does not insist on its own way; it is not irritable or resentful;

6. it does not rejoice at wrongdoing, but rejoices with the truth.

7. Love bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things.

8. Love never ends. As for prophecies, they will pass away; as for tongues, they will cease; as for knowledge, it will pass away.

9. For we know in part and we prophesy in part,

10. but when the perfect comes, the partial will pass away.

11. When I was a child, I spoke like a child, I thought like a child, I reasoned like a child. When I became a man, I gave up childish ways.

12. For now we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face. Now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I have been fully known.

13. So now faith, hope, and love abide, these three; but the greatest of these is love.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 13 is the heart of the spiritual gifts discussion and the theological center of the entire letter. It is the "more excellent way" announced in 12:31 — not a replacement for gifts but the environment in which gifts function as God intended.

**Verse 3 — Sinaiticus variant:** EMET flags the textual variant: Sinaiticus reads καυχήσωμαι ("that I may boast") rather than καυθήσομαι ("that I may be burned"). SOPHIA affirms the Sinaiticus reading: self-sacrifice for the sake of boasting is precisely the Corinthian disease. Even the most extreme act of self-giving, if motivated by pride rather than love, profits nothing. This reading fits the letter's sustained critique of boasting.

**Verses 4-7 — Love as anti-Corinth:** PNEUMA reads these verses as a mirror reflecting Corinthian failure. LOGOS identifies fifteen verbs. SOPHIA affirms: every attribute of love corresponds to a Corinthian vice. Love is patient — they are impatient. Love is not envious — they envy gifts. Love is not puffed up (οὐ φυσιοῦται) — they are inflated throughout the letter. Love does not insist on its own way — they insist on their rights (ch.8-10). Love does not rejoice at wrongdoing — they tolerated the man of chapter 5. Paul has written a portrait of love that is simultaneously a portrait of everything Corinth is not. The description is corrective, not merely inspirational.

**Verse 8 — The permanence of love:** LOGOS notes that tongues, prophecy, and knowledge — the three gifts the Corinthians most prize — are explicitly temporary. SOPHIA affirms: Paul's argument is eschatological. In the age to come, there will be no need for prophecy (God's presence will be unmediated), no need for tongues (communication will be direct), no need for partial knowledge (understanding will be complete). Only love survives the transition from this age to the next, because love is not a response to deficiency but the nature of God himself.

**Verse 12 — "Face to face":** EMET traces the allusion to Numbers 12:8. SOPHIA affirms: the "mirror" and "riddle" describe present theological knowledge — real but indirect, genuine but incomplete. The promise is not the abolition of knowledge but its fulfillment: "I shall know fully, even as I have been fully known." The passive "was fully known" (ἐπεγνώσθην) is crucial — God's knowledge of us is prior to and the ground of our knowledge of God. We are known before we know.

**Verse 13 — The triad and its order:** LOGOS asks why love is greatest. SOPHIA affirms: faith and hope are creaturely responses to divine action — faith trusts, hope anticipates. Love alone participates in God's own nature. Faith will give way to sight, hope to fulfillment — but love has no such limitation. Love does not await completion; it is itself the completed thing. "God is love" (1 John 4:8) — and what participates in God's nature endures forever.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
